data.title: This Is One Roach And Not An Infestation
data.description:
For those who have been predisposed to negativity, there was plenty to chew on yesterday. Amid a flurry of stellar bank earnings reports this week, there were two landmines. Zions Bancorp and Western Alliance Bancorp., which are both regional banks, disclosed that they were victims of fraud on loans they made to the same investment fund, which purchased distressed commercial mortgages. That sent shock waves through the financial sector, leading to a loss of 2.4% for the day. I seriously doubt this is an epidemic across the banking industry. To the contrary, it looks more like extremely poor due diligence and one bad actor. Regardless, investors sold everything related to the sector, and that weighed on all risk assets.

Before this news broke, there were already concerns about credit after subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings and auto-parts supplier First Brands Group filed for bankruptcy last month. That resulted in loan losses for JPMorgan and Fifth Third Bancorp. Despite what permabear Jamie Dimon might say, I think it's a mistake to extrapolate these cracks in credit across the entire lending landscape and conclude that the economy is on the cusp of a downturn. What we see are the weakest links in the chain break in concert with a deceleration in the rate of economic growth typical of a mid-cycle slowdown. We may have a cockroach here and there, but it is far from an infestation.

I can understand why the CEO of JPMorgan might feel differently, seeing as Jamie Dimon's bank took a $170 million loss when Tricolor collapsed. He doesn't look like the smartest guy in the room at this moment, which is why he tried to make it look more like an industry issue by saying that "when you see one cockroach, there's probably more." This is red meat for the bears who have come up empty-handed three years in a row, calling for an end to the economic expansion and bull market.

We have needed a pullback of 3-5%, at a minimum, to cool off this red-hot market. Renewed trade tensions with China, a government shutdown, and assertions that a bubble may be forming in the AI sector couldn't instigate it. It looks like credit concerns, even if misplaced, may finally do it. Yet these isolated loan losses are not end-of-cycle symptoms, as those who have been pessimistic purport them to be. Consumers are the backbone of the economy, and they collectively still look to be on firm ground.

While the softening jobs market has been a legitimate concern, I see this as having more to do with immigration and trade policies than economic weakness. Real consumer spending and personal income growth look steady, and household balance sheets remain in good shape. Total household debt as a percentage of disposable income at 89.5% is as low as we have seen since the mid-1990s. As I pointed out yesterday, debt service payments as a percentage of disposable income remain below pre-pandemic levels. The bottom line is that the economy should continue to grow next year, as should corporate earnings, which is what fuels a bull market.

The good news from recent events is that it has rebuilt a wall of worry for the market to climb. With respect to the most recent survey from the American Association of Individual Investors (AAII), I was encouraged to see the percentage who think the stock market will be higher in the next six months plunge from nearly 46% to a below average 33.7%.

While volatility is likely to increase between now and month's end, for the first time in a long time, this pullback in the broad market should create some buying opportunities for astute investors in stocks and sectors that become oversold. Strong corporate profits combined with another rate cut from the Fed should be followed by an easing in trade tensions with China, a reopening of the federal government, and recognition that today's credit concerns are overblown.

data.title: Charles Schwab: Strong Results, Nice Growth
data.description:
Article Thesis

The Charles Schwab Corporation (NYSE:SCHW) reported its most recent earnings results on Thursday, showing compelling growth and once again beating estimates. The outlook for the company remains positive, I believe, and its valuation is not very high considering both its quality and its growth momentum, which is why I remain bullish on the company.

Past Coverage

I have written about The Charles Schwab Corporation here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in July 2025, around one quarter ago. Since then, Charles Schwab's shares have not moved much, but with the company announcing its most recent earnings results on Thursday, it is time to update my thesis.

What Happened?

Thursday, before the market's opening, The Charles Schwab Corporation announced the following earnings results:

We see that the company beat estimates on both lines, which, of course, is a nice result. This was not too much of a surprise, however, as the company did the same during the four quarters before the most recent quarter, too -- analysts have a tendency to underestimate the company and its growth momentum, at least over the last year or so.

The revenue beat was relatively minor, in the 2% range, while earnings per share were about 5% higher than what was forecasted, which is a pretty meaningful beat, I believe.

Shares nevertheless traded down on Thursday, which is likely the result of a broader pullback for the entire market on the back of macro issues such as the current shutdown, trade worries with China, etc.

Charles Schwab's Performance In Q3: Pretty Strong

Generally, companies from the financial industry aren't seen as high-growth investments -- and that makes sense, as the industry is not showing any tech-like growth rates at all. Still, Charles Schwab delivered a very strong revenue increase of 27% for the most recent quarter, relative to one year earlier, which is a better growth rate compared to what many of the Magnificent 7 are showing in the recent past -- NVIDIA (NVDA) is the exception. Charles Schwab's revenue growth during the third quarter was thus quite strong, being driven by several factors.

The company saw the amount of client assets grow around 17% versus one year earlier, with client assets hitting a new record high of $11.6 trillion during the third quarter. More client assets means, all else equal, more trading revenue and fee income for Charles Schwab, thus this is a major driver of its revenue growth. Client assets partially grew due to a positive equity market performance, with the S&P 500 (SPY) rising by around 13% over the last twelve months. But client assets also benefitted from positive inflows, with core net new assets of close to $140 billion for the third quarter alone. During the year-to-date period, core net new assets totaled more than $350 billion, showing that Charles Schwab continues to attract new money at a very nice pace, while the acceleration between H1 and Q3 -- H1 had an average of around $110 billion per quarter -- indicates positive momentum. If this positive momentum holds, we could see even better net new assets growth during the current quarter. Depending on how the market performs in Q4, we could thus see new record-high client assets towards the end of the year, although this most likely won't happen in case equity markets pull back in a major way.

Charles Schwab also generates revenue via net interest income, for which there are two variables: interest received and interest expenses. The former was almost flat year-over-year, rising by just 1%, but Charles Schwab was able to cut its interest expenses massively. Interest expense almost dropped by 50%, from $1.71 billion to $910 million, which was a big $800 million tailwind for its net interest income. This was possible thanks to Charles Schwab replacing higher-cost debt with lower-cost debt, thanks to client transactional sweep cash growing by a nice $13.5 billion, allowing the company to reduce its more expensive bank funding by a relatively comparable $13 billion. If this trend continues, Charles Schwab should be able to lower its interest expenses further in the coming quarters, which should add nicely to its revenues -- although this won't be a tailwind forever, of course, as interest expenses can't fall forever. Still, there is further potential for this mechanism to work in the company's favor in the foreseeable future, I believe.

Charles Schwab did quite well when it comes to adding new customers, with Q3 being the fourth quarter in a row of more than 1 million new brokerage accounts being added. While neobrokers have gotten a lot of attention in recent years, it looks like many consumers still value what more traditional brokers are offering -- with Charles Schwab being a clear winner. The company reports that it was recognized as the leader in both education and service by Kiplinger during the third quarter, which could be a major factor for why customers flock to Charles Schwab -- many value a broker with good service. If Charles Schwab continues to keep up the good work when it comes to managing its relations with its customers well, and when customers remain happy with what they get from Charles Schwab, then I would not be surprised to see the company show a nice growth rate in the number of new accounts in the foreseeable future, too, which would make a nice basis for future client asset growth and thus growing trading and fee income.

On the profitability side, Charles Schwab's Q3 was a major success: its business model has low proportional costs, so when revenues climb, that oftentimes means growing margins -- operating leverage works in SCHW's favor. During the third quarter, Charles Schwab was able to turn an already strong 27% revenue increase into an even better 61% net income increase, thanks to its pre-tax profit margin soaring to 51%, up by a hefty 1,000 basis points over the last year. Looking at earnings per share, things were even better, as Charles Schwab benefitted from a declining share count thanks to its share repurchases -- earnings per share thus were up by 70% over the last year on an adjusted basis, rising to $1.31. Charles Schwab continued to repurchase shares during the third quarter, spending $2.7 billion on share repurchases in Q3, which is equal to around 1.6% of the company's current market capitalization, or a little more than 6% on an annualized basis. With these buybacks, Charles Schwab reduced the share count by 29 million in Q3 (not accounting for some shares being issued to employees and management, though), thus I expect that Q4 will show a nice earnings per share tailwind from buybacks as well.

Is Charles Schwab A Good Investment?

As shown above, Charles Schwab did very well during the third quarter: it added more clients, clients added new money, the company benefitted from a positive equity market performance, was able to reduce its high-cost debt, and last but not least, its business model allows it to benefit a lot from operating leverage when its business expands, which is why margins soared. In the near term, these trends should more or less remain in place, I believe, although reducing interest expenses won't be possible forever.

Charles Schwab is forecasted to see its earnings per share climb by 17% next year and by 17% in 2027, too, which would be quite appealing. Today, The Charles Schwab Corporation trades at a little less than 20x net profits, which does not seem like an expensive valuation at all for a company growing as fast as Charles Schwab does. I thus remain bullish on the company and believe shareholders should do well when they buy SCHW at a little less than 20x this year's profits if the company keeps growing at a very nice pace for the foreseeable future.

data.title: Enbridge: Recent AI News And Rate Cuts Are Very Bullish For Energy Infrastructure
data.description:
It’s not a secret that Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) is one of my favorite energy infrastructure companies due to it being a triple threat in the sector. ENB is operating a 18,085 miles of liquids pipelines, 69,852 miles of natural gas midstream pipelines and transmission assets, while operating 110,606 miles of gas transmission, transportation and distribution lines with 64,453 miles of services lines in it’s gas utility company across North America. ENB also operates a renewable energy portfolio that exceeds $8 billion of assets that generates 7,212 megawatts (MW) gross of zero-emission energy. ENB has built a portfolio of complimentary assets that makes it vertically integrated, collecting revenue the entire value chain rather than just acting as a tollbooth with its midstream infrastructure. The A.I. revolution has been kicked up a notch as OpenAI has contracted with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO) for 26 gigawatts of chips over the past several weeks which is ushering in a new wave of infrastructure being built. On BlackRock’s Q3 earnings call they outlined that their estimating that $1.5 trillion of capital is going to be needed in the next 5 years just for the core and shell’s of new data centers and this funding doesn’t include the capital needed for chips. The opportunity for energy infrastructure companies is massive, and ENB’s assets will be critical as not only will they be transporting energy throughout the country but ENB’s utility companies will see increased electricity demand from all the new infrastructure that is brought online. AS the Fed reduces rates it will cause the cost of capital to decline which could help ENB’s margins expand while making the 5.64% dividend yield look even more enticing. I am very bullish on ENB and believe it will provide both capital appreciation and recurring income going forward.

Following up on my previous email about Enbridge

At the beginning of August I wrote an article about ENB (can be read here) where I discussed how I thought shares would break out to the upside while continuing to increase the dividend on an annualized basis. Shares of ENB appreciated by 7.72% to $50.43 then retraced -6.43% to $47.29. ENB is up 0.83% since the beginning of august and after the dividend is accounted for the total return is 2.28%. I had discussed that ENB’s diversified infrastructure and irreplicable moat position it to benefit from rising energy demand as they continued to increase the amount of profitability generated from their operating segments. I believed that ENB was a compelling investment as the amount of energy needed to sustain an increased number of data centers would drive upstream production, midstream transportation, and utilization of electricity. I am upgrading my investment thesis to very bullish on ENB as their in a position to benefit across the board from increased energy demand and a lower rate environment. ENB is likely to continue higher while increasing the dividend YoY as the income generated from the risk-free environment declines.

The Datacenter landscape has dramatically changed in the past several weeks and it is incredibly bullish for Enbridge

The largest concentration of data centers runs along ENB’s pipeline infrastructure through the east coast and down through Texas. ENB’s strategic acquisition to acquire The East Ohio Gas Company, Questar Gas Company, and Public Service Company of North Carolina in 2023 from Dominion (D) allowed them to create North America's largest natural gas utility platform delivering power to more than 7 million customers. This has paid off in spades for ENB considering the Public Service Company of North Carolina has utility assets in the largest concentration of data centers in the country. The digital economy is running on electricity and the rate at which data center expansion is occurring is only accelerating the amount of electricity needed. ENB is in a unique position because their operating a premier natural gas transportation network throughout North America that allows them to act as a toll road across the continent. As each data center comes online the demand for natural gas-fired power increases which is likely to cause larger amounts of natural gas volume being contracted for transportation through ENB. ENB’s strategic acquisition in 2023 now allows them to directly benefit once the natural gas reaches the power plants as they are now supplying the electricity where the largest concentration of data centers are.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) has highlighted that data centers are shaping commercial demand for energy, which is now projected to grow faster than other sectors due to their 24/7 load profiles. In the short term, the EIA is projecting that the demand for energy is going to continue to increase through the end of 2026. The narrative about transitioning to 100% renewables has been pushed to the side as we need more energy from all sources to meet the energy demands of tomorrow. As more data centers come online and the global demand for energy increases there will be an increased need for American oil and gas. ENB has a buisness model that is largely fee-based as it gets paid for the volume of gas it transports in the midstream segment which insulates them from the volatility of commodity prices. This allows ENB to create a more predictable stream of revenue that is becoming more tied to the structural growth in power demand. I think that the market is going to realize that the A.I. and data center boom isn’t a short term occurrence and that there is a multi-decade technological shift occurring that will require additional amounts of transportation and distribution of energy. All of the recent news over the past 3-weeks is an indication of the additional power that will be needed in the future.

Over the past several weeks there has been a tremendous amount of news pertaining to data centers that is bullish for ENB. Microsoft (MSFT) recently delivered a data center announcement, where they are going to spend $4 billion and claim that it will be the most advanced in the world. Next OpenAI and NVDA announced a strategic partnership to deploy 10 gigawatts of NVDA systems and buy directly from NVDA rather than sourcing their compute from 3rd parties. OpenAI also announced a deal with AMD where they would deploy 6 gigawatts of their next-generation chips starting in the 2nd half of 2026. OpenAI then announced a 10-gigawatt deal with AVGO for custom AI accelerators. OpenAI will design the accelerators and systems, then AVGO will developed and deploy them. This goes to show that companies such as OpenAI can no longer rely on sourcing their compute from 3rd parties alone and need to own and operate data centers while still buying compute on the secondary market to accomplish their goals. I believe that were going to see the hyperscalers make some big announcements during the Q3 conference calls because they can’t afford to fall behind in the A.I. race. While all roads from a hardware perspective lead back to NVDA and the semi’s, to power these data centers all roads are leading back to ENB from the natural gas to the electrification of these areas.

Why I am upgrading my investment thesis on ENB

The Fed has finally cut rates for the first time in 2025 and CME Group is expecting that we will get 2 more cuts this year. This has caused the 3-month to the 10-year treasury note to all fall under a 4% yield making ENB’s dividend much more attractive. This is beneficial for ENB from 2 perspectives. ENB currently has $71.24 billion in long-term debt on the balance sheet and is paying $3.07 billion in annualized interest expenses to service the debt. As rates continue to fall ENB will be able to refinance their debt obligations and increase their margins. ENB has also increased their dividend for the past 30-years with a 9% dividend CAGR since 1995. ENB is expecting to continue increasing the dividend at an average rate of 3% annually through 2026 then increasing this to 5% after the 2026 fiscal year. Investors are able to lock in a current dividend of $2.69 which is a 5.64% yield in an interest rate environment that is expected to continue significantly lower over the next 2-years. With more than $7.48 trillion sitting in money market accounts I believe capital is going to start coming back into the equity markets as income investors become yield starved on the sidelines. ENB should be an attractive investment as it has established a multi-decade track record of growing the dividend while outpacing the risk-free rate of return.

ENB continues to focus its strategic priorities for capital allocation around its balance sheet, return of capital, and generating future growth. To do this ENB is maintaining a Debt-to-EBITDA metric of 4.7x which is in the midpoint of their optimal range of 4.5x to 5.0x. This provides ENB with the ability to deploy roughly $9 - $10 billion of annual investment capacity. This will allow ENB to prioritize brownfield opportunities and utility-like growth projects which allow ENB to scale where their competitors can’t. ENB delivered a Q2 record with $4.6 billion in Adjusted EBITDA which was a 7% YoY increase while confirming their 2025 guidance. ENB is expecting their Adjusted EBITDA to grow at a 7-9% range YoY through 2026 while their adjusted earnings per share increases 4-6%. ENB will be in a position to continue enhancing its profitability through improved system utilization, asset life extension, and reliability. ENB is in a great position to capitalize on the growing power demand as they generate increased levels of revenue and profitability that can be reinvested back into the business.

ENB and the rest of the energy infrastructure sector had a strong 2022 due to the winter storms that hit the southwest. ENB’s strategic investments have allowed them to significantly grow in the TTM. Over the past 3 ½ years ENB’s revenue has increased by 27.17% ($10.11 billion) to $47.34 billion. ENB’s gross profit has increased by 33.49% ($4.86 billion) to $19.39 billion while its EBITDA has grown by 32.56% ($3.05 billion) to $12.4 billion. When I look at how ENB is allocating capital, and how much the hyperscalers are spending on data centers I am becoming more and more bullish on energy infrastructure. ENB’s future endeavors should allow them to grow their earnings and increase the dividend for many years to come and I believe that they are currently undervalued. Shares of Oklo Inc. (OKLO) just reached a market cap of $25 billion because The U.S. Department of Energy selected them and 3 other companies for its new pilot program to build advanced nuclear fuel lines. The key word is pilot program and the reality is that OKLO is a pre-revenue company that isn’t expected to produce a profit until 2030. I think that ENB will catch a bid as they are ready now to provide critical infrastructure to the data center surge today.

Risks to investing in ENB

While I am bullish on ENB there are several risk factors to consider. Investors should do their own due diligence as my investment thesis could be wrong. If the CapEx buildout of data centers slows down it will likely hurt the investment case for ENB as there will be less energy needed in the future. This would cause the entire sector to decline in value and eliminate a lot of upside opportunity. We could also see new technological innovations that overhaul the efficiency in fossil fuels which cause utilization rates to decline. If this occurs less fuel would move through ENB’s system and their DCF and dividends would come under pressure. If the Fed decides to halt their rate cuts then ENB could find itself with less prospects to increase margins as refinancing debt may not be an option. Investors should do their own research and see if ENB is a good investment for them

What I am looking for in ENB's Q3 Earnings

ENB is coming off a strong Q2 where they produced record amounts of EBITDA and beat analyst estimates on the top and bottom line. The solid performance was largely driven by the strength in its gas transmission and gas distribution and storage segments. ENB reaffirmed its full-year 2025 guidance and believes it will reach the upper end of its adjusted EBITDA forecast of $19.4 billion to $20.0 billion. In Q3 I am looking for the gas transmission and distribution segment to be a key driver of growth. I want to see details about throughput levels on its natural gas pipelines and any updates on the integration of its recently acquired US gas utilities. This is critical for their future guidance as data center demand is expected to drive their future capital deployment and earnings potential. I also want to see updates on their progress for capital projects and if any will be added due to the additional data center buildout that has been announced. If ENB tops $0.41 in EPS and $11.9 billion in revenue it will demonstrate the strength to its business model and that it is on its way to achieving its financial projections.

Conclusion

I’m very excited about ENB because there are many areas of the market making new highs and energy infrastructure has been left behind. None of the future data center build out can happen without more energy and ENB is structured to benefit across the entire value chain. ENB’s organic growth projects will support increased utilization rates throughout its business segments while operating a business that is next to impossible to be replicated. As the risk-free rate of return continues to decline ENB’s dividend will look much more attractive and I believe income investors will flock toward its 5.64% yield and 3-decade long track record of increasing its annual dividend. I believe that energy infrastructure is going to become more valuable as the demand for energy increases as ENB’s infrastructure can’t be replicated. I am upgrading my investment thesis to very bullish and I believe ENB will finish the year strong.

data.title: Fossil Group Offers Great Upside, But Not Without Great Risk
data.description:
The last couple of days have been a wild time for shareholders of watch company Fossil Group (NASDAQ:FOSL). The business saw its share price surge 41.5% on October 15th after rumors began circulating that it could be pursuing an IPO for its Indian operations. But then, just as quickly as the stock rose, it came back down, with the stock plunging 29.6% on October 16th. This was driven by the revelation that the company is not, in fact, currently planning such a maneuver. Instead, at least for now, it seems intent on pursuing its existing restructuring plan.

This plan has been a messy affair for the business. Revenue has been declining for the last few years now as management purposely shrinks the company. The firm's belief is that it has overextended itself. And now, over the next few years, it hopes to shrink itself considerably so that it can achieve profitability. On top of this, the company is going through a rather interesting debt restructuring that could help to extend its debt maturities and raise additional capital. But unfortunately, this will increase costs even while management continues to push for meaningful cost reductions.

If we assume that management can achieve its targets for the next few years, there could be some upside under the right circumstances. The business certainly has some things going for it when it comes to industry conditions. But at the same time, there are a lot of unknowns here and a lot of risk that investors are assuming in order to capture these returns. It is entirely possible that this play will go on to generate significant market beating potential. But because of how risky everything is at the moment, I think the best thing to do is to downgrade the company from the ‘hold’ I assigned it in February of 2022 to a ‘sell’ today.

Let’s talk turkey… I mean India

The crazy price movements that Fossil Group exhibited over the last couple of days began in early trading on October 15th the news broke that the enterprise was evaluating a potential IPO of its India based subsidiary known as Fossil India. The objective here was to raise between $300 million and $400 million by selling up to a 25% ownership stake in that business. Considering that Fossil Group has a market capitalization today of $142 million and an enterprise value of $211.1 million, that would imply that there is upside potential for shareholders. It wouldn't be shocking to see the stock double, triple, or even more, if this development ended up being true.

Truly, the market opportunity in India is significant. In an article that I published last year regarding technology behemoth Apple (AAPL), I talked about certain demographic trends in India. Specifically, I pointed out how the population of the country would grow from 1.43 billion in 2023 to 1.68 billion by the year 2075. It would only be sometime after that when population would be expected to fall. This comes at a time when China's population is already declining. In fact, by the end of this century, China's population is expected to fall to 771.3 million, which is a far cry from the 1.41 billion the country had back in 2023.

Just like when China began growing rapidly and became a huge player on the world stage, this growth for India should usher in significant wealth and a growing upper and middle class. Last year, about 432 million individuals in India were middle class. Some sources have this growing to 500 million by the end of this decade. It shouldn't be a surprise, then, that this growth would result in significant demand for certain goods. According to a report issued by Deloitte last year, about 60% of consumers in India spend some amount of money on luxury goods. They define this as luxury leather goods, eyewear, watches, jewelry, fashion products, and cosmetics. And the group that makes these purchases mostly consists of younger generations. That same study said that luxury spending in India can be expected to grow from $7 billion last year to $30 billion by the year 2030. And it is their assertion that the watch industry could be a major beneficiary of this expansion.

Naturally, this could be a tantalizing opportunity for a company such as Fossil Group. But sadly, there doesn't seem to be much upside here based on the data available. For starters, as the chart above illustrates, the number of stores that the business has an operation has declined in recent years. Much of this decline has involved the Americas and Europe. But even in India, which involves its Indian operations, as well as operations in China and elsewhere, experienced a decline, with the number of locations dropping from 80 in 2022 to 61 in the first half of this year. Data is hard to come by regarding its Indian operations. One source reported that, in the fiscal year ending in March of 2024, Fossil India brought in around $98 million of sales. Try as I might, I could not verify this data as accurate.

The other issue is that, contrary to the rumors, there seems to be no truth behind the idea that the business is currently pursuing an IPO for any of its subsidiaries. The company acknowledged as much in a regulatory filing made on October 16th. And that's almost certainly what caused the plunge in its share price after the huge run higher. Even if the company changes its mind, I struggle to imagine that it could get anywhere near the reported figures for a 25% equity stake in such a small piece of itself. If we were seeing rapid growth in India associated with the company’s operations, that might be a different story. But with the number of stores closing throughout Asia, that seems improbable.

A restructuring is occurring

Although there is no evidence that the rumors in question are true, with management even going so far as to refute them, there could be some potential here. But first, we should talk about what all is going on with the business. Management has been working hard to restructure the business. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the last three completed fiscal years. And in the chart below, you can see figures for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time last year. There is a lot to unpack here though.

Obviously, the reduction in the number of locations that it has in operation have played a role here. And as the locations for the institution declined, we saw weakness across all of its sales categories. The chart below illustrates as much, with revenue associated with watches dropping from $1.31 billion in 2022 to only $897.5 million in 2024. For the first half of this year, the $368.5 million in revenue generated from these operations fell short of the $397.9 million reported the same time last year. Leathers, jewelry, and other products, all reported similar declines.

In fact, the company talks a great deal about its turnaround plan in its latest investor presentation, which came out earlier this year. In addition to refocusing on certain core operations, including leveraging certain licensed brands and launching a new FOSSIL Brand Platform, the company is also fighting hard to optimize its global wholesale footprint, to sell off certain non-core assets, and to improve margins as much as possible. In fact, for this year alone, the company has been pushing to achieve $100 million in selling, general, and administrative savings compared to what it saw last year. And sure enough, we have seen some progress there. Profitability and cash flows have improved in the first half of this year relative to the same time last year, even as the company prepares for a $45 million reduction in revenue specifically tied to store closures.

The firm is making multiple changes to how it operates during this process. For instance, it's changing how it approaches e-commerce by reducing promotions that drive higher quality traffic while also increasing average unit retail. It's also integrating the Buy with Prime features that Amazon (AMZN) has onto its own website. And when it comes to its retail operations, even though the company is targeting 50 store closures this year, it continues to focus on things like improving personalization and making changes to their store operating model. The firm is especially interested in emphasizing traditional watches, which it believes could be a higher return opportunity than the alternative.

Management's plan is to eventually shrink the company to the point where it can generate around $800 million in revenue by the year 2027. Once the company hits this target, it expects to achieve adjusted operating income margin of somewhere in the mid to high single digit range. This compares to the negative low single digits that the company expects for this year. It also expects to finally be free cash flow positive by 2027, if not earlier.

To illustrate for you what the ultimate impact of achieving this target for 2027 might be, I created the table above. In it, you can see a calculation for net profits, operating cash flow, and EBITDA for the company if it hits $800 million in revenue and achieves an adjusted operating margin of between 5% and 9%. Other than some changes the company is currently pursuing with its existing debt, I'm assuming no changes in its balance sheet. But of course, net cash outflows in the near term probably will cause some alterations here.

Speaking of debt, management has been working hard on essentially refinancing the largest chunk of the $179 million of debt that it has on its books right now. That refers to the $150 million of notes coming due next year. On October 16th, the company issued a press release wherein it mentioned that it is announcing yet another extension of an exchange offer for these unsecured senior notes. According to the terms of the proposal, if the company can get at least 90% of the holders of those notes, in terms of outstanding principal balance, to agree, it will essentially swap these out for new secured notes that won't be due until 2029.

The company has been using a carrot and stick method to achieve this change. For starters, for any market participants who would be willing to pitch in additional capital amounting to $32.5 million, it will provide notes that will carry a 9.5% annual interest rate as opposed to the 7% that the existing notes offer. And for the minority of debt holders who are not pitching in additional capital, the offer to tender their notes brings with it an increase in interest rate from 7% to 7.5%.

The October 16th press release detailed how 75.37% of all debt holders for these notes have tendered them. But that's not enough in order to force a change. So instead, the company is heading to court in order to muscle this change through. This will bring in additional capital for the company and it will simultaneously extend the maturity of these notes. But unfortunately, it will mean an extra $2.9 million in interest expense annually. And that's excluding the new capital coming in. With that factored in as well, we can expect annual interest expense to jump by roughly $6 million. For a company that continues to struggle with profits and cash flows, that is an issue in and of itself. But based on my own understanding of what is going on, this move will likely be approved by the courts. So at least near term solvency issues will be resolved.

Another thing that I can say is definitely positive about the company is that the American watch industry is showing signs of stabilizing. In the chart above, you can see an index made by WatchCharts that shows how watches on the secondary market are performing from a price perspective. You can imagine this as being like the S&P 500 is for stocks. After seeing a spike a couple of years ago because of disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a plunge in pricing. That certainly didn't help companies such as Fossil Group. But now, we are starting to see a bit of an improvement. However, with economic conditions worsening, I am hesitant to say that this recent turnaround in pricing will continue.

Globally, management believes that the opportunity for the watch industry is massive and growing. In 2024 it was worth $82 billion. By 2028 it’s expected to grow to $100 billion, which indicates an annualized growth rate of 4.1%. In some countries, like the US, that rate will be slower, with annualized upside of 3.4%. Going back to India for a moment, management is far more optimistic. They currently forecast annualized growth there of 9.5%.

Shares offer potential… if things work out

Using the estimates that I provided for the 2027 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares of Fossil Group are valued. Seeing the company trade at low single digits or mid single digits from a trading multiple perspective is fantastic. But this doesn't necessarily mean that upside exists. In the first chart below, you can see how much upside or downside the company should experience between now and 2027 if it can achieve its targets with the low end of its adjusted operating margin range, which I consider to be a 5% margin, plays out.

If the company trades at a lower multiple, which is not unrealistic, we could see the stock fall by as much as 28.6%. But of course, if we apply higher multiples, the picture changes significantly. In the same chart, you can see the same type of analysis under the assumption that it achieves an adjusted operating margin of 9%. This would indicate meaningful upside. But in the last the company trades at multiples of 10 or higher, the payoff really won't be worth it. This means that there is significant risk relative to the potential upside that exists right now. And that is not something I am comfortable with.

Takeaway

Right now, I would say that investors should be very cautious. There is certainly a lot of potential here for Fossil Group to do incredibly well. But this entire plan of shrinking its operations creates a lot of potential downside. On top of this, the revelation that a public offering for its Indian subsidiary is not likely to occur in the foreseeable future serves as a blow to investors. While there are some positives here, including potential growth opportunities in India and a stabilization of the market here at home, that's not enough, even with the potential significant upside, to justify the risk in my view. And it's because of that that I have the firm rated a ‘sell’ until greater clarity comes about.

data.title: Why I'm Buying Old-School Dividends: 2 Reliable Picks Up To 10% Yields
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

I know some people who love to have the shiniest new things. A new technology comes out, and they'll pay a huge premium to be the first to have the newest and fanciest. Whether it's TV, cars, or cell phones, they want the newest.

If you have a passion for something and want to be on the cutting edge, great. Although if you are one of those people, you are probably painfully aware that the very first versions of things are often a bit rough around the edges. In a couple of years, newer versions will come out that resolve a lot of the problems that the people who bought the first version complain about. That's a trade-off you make when you are buying the newest, also known as the least tested.

If you've been following me, then you know that I've been pounding the table about fixed-income opportunities for the past few years as the Fed raised rates. This is for a very simple reason – interest rates are high.

When you are investing in fixed-income, higher interest rates mean you're getting a higher yield. It isn't a new idea, based on some slick technology that has some great potential. While the underlying companies might be doing all sorts of new and innovative things, investing in debt or in preferred equity provides a more predictable and reliable return.

Today, I want to take a look at two different fixed-income investments.

  • One is a fund that invests in convertible bonds. These bonds provide exposure to the upside in the equity. If the company turns out to be as great as the common equity investors think, convertible bonds can provide an outsized return. On the other hand, it is a debt, and the principal is a legal obligation for the company. That provides some downside protection in the event that the company isn't as great as expected.
  • The second is a preferred equity option in a telecom company. A nice, boring business that provides steady cash flow. Preferred equity provides a fixed-rate return, and it trades at a discount to par primarily because interest rates are high.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: CCD – Yield 10.7%

Convertible bonds offer a means to raise non-dilutive capital at a very low borrowing cost. Moreover, the interest payments on convertible notes are tax- deductible, making these highly lucrative for growth-focused companies to raise capital. Most convertible notes carry 0% coupon (or a very low value), and these securities can conditionally convert to common shares. Due to this correlation with common stock prices, convertible notes are well-positioned in a bull run, with soaring valuations and high IPO volumes.

According to a recent Reuters report, global convertible bond issuances have reached five-year highs this year, only behind the post-pandemic bull market of 2021. Companies are seizing the opportunity to borrow free money in exchange for warrants, giving investors the option to convert into common stock based on pre-determined growth targets. Source

The U.S. IPO market is also hot, with 165 transactions in 1H 2025 (annualized to 330 in the chart below). Barring the anomaly created during the pandemic, we see a steady uptrend in IPOs. Source

It isn’t just that. Recent IPOs in 2025 have achieved strong price performance, unlike that seen in recent years. The collective sentiment bodes well for convertible issuances, as companies seek to use their valuation and growth story to their advantage. About 60% of all issued convertibles are denominated in U.S. Dollars, and not surprisingly, the IT sector is the largest issuer, comprising a third of the units outstanding.

Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund (CCD) is a CEF focused on convertible notes, with a massively diversified portfolio of 610 holdings. Despite volatile market circumstances since CCD’s IPO in 2015, the fund has done a good job maintaining its Net Asset Value over this timeframe while distributing large income on a monthly basis.

YTD, CCD’s NAV is up 10%, while price has been trending downward, presenting an opportunity to lock in a high yield. CCD has made steady monthly distributions since its IPO in 2016, with a notable payment raise in 2021. The CEF’s $0.195/share calculates to a high 10.7% yield.

CCD operates with a 33% leverage, to amplify its returns from active management of convertibles. The CEF’s borrowings carry a floating-rate of SOFR + 0.65%, positioning the fund for reducing interest expenses from rate cuts of any magnitude. CCD presents a well-managed fund to take advantage of the industry tailwinds and market dynamics.

Pick #2: TDS Preferreds – Up To 7.9% Yield

Telephone & Data Systems (TDS) is a Fortune 1000 company providing internet and phone services to 5.5 million consumers in urban, suburban, and rural communities across America. TDS is a majority shareholder (83%) in Array Digital Infrastructure (AD), formerly known as USCellular. AD owns ~4,400 cell towers, and is integral for the deployment of 5G and other wireless tech across the country. Source

Following the sale of wireless assets to T-Mobile, AD received $2.6 billion in cash proceeds. The company paid a $23/share special dividend to shareholders, and TDS was a significant beneficiary. TDS however, doesn’t intend to distribute this $1.6 billion windfall; instead, the company plans to use the proceeds to reduce debt and expand their operational footprint. We also note that AD is awaiting the completion of spectrum transactions with AT&T and Verizon, and these are expected to close in 2H 2025 and Q3 2026, respectively. As a result, AD is not providing any financial guidance for 2025.

On its own merit, TDS Telecom is sustainably profitable, with $522 million in total operating revenues during 1H 2025. This generated $165 million in Adjusted EBITDA during the first half of the year, and the company ended Q2 with ~$150 million in cash and cash equivalents.

We will note that as a combined entity, TDS is required to report the cash and cash equivalents by including those held in AD’s balance sheet. However, TDS does not directly have access to this cash, not unless AD decides to pay a dividend to its shareholders. Source

According to the company's Q2 report, about 26% of TDS’ residential broadband connections support speeds in excess of 1Gbps (up from 19% last year), and the company deployed to 27,000 new marketable fiber addresses during the quarter. Management noted that the company is on track for 150,000 new marketable fiber addresses this year.

Most investors don’t read TDS financial filings closely to recognize the nuances of the company’s reporting segments. There are three sections – (1) - TDS Consolidated, the full business, including the reports from Array Digital; (2) Array Digital; (3) TDS TELECOM. We mainly care about TDS Telecom, since the TDS preferred shares are under their custodianship. If you still have doubts, take a look at the data from the filing itself. Source

TDS Telecom spent $2 million on interest expenses during the first six months of 2025, and the company is GAAP profitable. TDS has affirmed its guidance for the full year, and the company expects Adj. EBITDA between $320 - $360 million. TDS preferreds cost the company $17 million every quarter, which is adequately covered by the company’s earnings and liquidity levels. We note that management has very specifically mentioned that they do not intend to redeem the preferreds anytime soon. The company sees the preferreds as a foundational capital, and is more focused on investing in asset expansion instead.

  • 6.625% Deposit Shares, Series UU Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (TDS.PR.U) – Yield 7.8%

  • 6.00% Deposit Shares, Series VV Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (TDS.PR.V) – Yield 7.9%

This bodes well for the income investor in me, who wants to sit back and collect big qualified dividends. TDS-V currently presents an attractive investment, with a 7.9% yield and ~30% upside to par.

Conclusion

We've seen preferred equity and bonds start seeing prices recover as the rate-cutting cycle has resumed. There are still plenty of opportunities out there to generate strong yields. As rates decline, those opportunities will get harder to find. But harder does not mean impossible.

While the market chases the next hot thing at +20 price/earnings and a sub 3% yield, we'll keep finding options that yield +7%.

Happy dividend collecting!

data.title: UBS Still A Critical Bank To Hold, As Credit Suisse Integration Finalizes
data.description:
The Stock: Major Swiss/Global Bank On A Bullish Course Since Credit Suisse Merger

For today's article I am hopping across the pond to Europe and covering a significant Swiss bank, UBS (NYSE:UBS), whose shares also trade on the NYSE in the US as well.

After recently attending a regional conference in southern Europe where banking innovation and fintech were key topics, my interest in the banking sector renewed after some time and I think it is time to follow up on this stock I covered exactly a year ago, so you can call this a 1 year anniversary article.

Since my October 2024 coverage when I recommended holding on to this stock, it has surged nearly +24%.

At the time, I was positive on the firm's future potential growth from its fee-driven business segments, but I also thought it was somewhat overvalued at the time. Apparently, the market was even more confident, as momentum data shows this stock outperforming the S&P500 index over the last year.

Based on my updated research today, and a somewhat updated methodology, interestingly I came up with the exact same rating again of hold. Today's article argues that a neutral view is the best course on UBS after considering multiple upside/downside factors impacting the top and bottom lines, balance sheet, and price expectations.

The firm's greatest strength, from my view, is top-notch credit ratings and being a closely-watched systemically critical global bank we can expect it to undergo strict scrutiny on capital requirements, which I think is a good thing.

Rating Score: Hold

Growth Drivers: Certain Segments Driving Upside, As Innovation/AI Also Play A Role

First, we should talk about some growth drivers both from the perspective of the overall sector but also company-specific factors.

The role of AI and innovation cannot be ignored when talking about banking today. UBS even reiterated they are "investing in our future infrastructure and AI," in their Q2 results presentation.

A bank continually innovating can be an upside, particularly if those innovations can make processes more cost-efficient while also improving the client experience, not sacrificing it.

When thinking about the sector, UBS is in a niche focused on 3 segments: wealth, asset management, and investment banking. For instance, on its official website it boasts of its "tailored wealth solutions for families and entrepreneurs to achieve individual financial goals."

I think, therefore, an impact to future growth will come from demand for personalized wealth advisory. A study last February by global consultancy McKinsey & Co pointed to expected continued demand in the US market especially:

Looking ahead, the number of advised relationships will continue to grow; we estimate it could reach 67 million to 71 million by 2034, a 28 to 34 percent increase from 53 million relationships in 2024.

UBS, although based in Switzerland, has penetrated the US market for a long time now, particularly in wealth advisory, and in Barron's list of top private wealth management teams in the US for 2025, UBS-affiliated advisors had 5 of the top 30 spots, but the list also makes it clear that there is stiff competition from peers like Morgan Stanley (MS) and Merrill.

From a public policy and regulatory standpoint, because of the sheer size and systemically-critical role played by UBS, Swiss authorities in its home country have been tightening rules, as reported in Reuters just a few weeks ago:

The government has proposed stricter measures for UBS to avoid a repeat of the Credit Suisse meltdown and to shield taxpayers from losses.

I think this could be seen as an upside factor since it could lessen some future downside risks such a bank could face and increase capital requirements.

In the asset management space, I am looking for new client monies coming to the firm, as this can drive future fees income. Unfortunately, Q2 results reported net outflows of $2B:

Net new money (2.0bn), driven by net outflows in Fixed Income (excluding money market) and Multi-asset & Solutions, partially offset by net inflows in ETFs.

At the investment banking unit, there was better news, as it saw +13% YoY revenue growth:

In particular, UBS mentioned this was driven by the Global Markets business which includes activities like execution services, derivatives, and financing, all of which saw momentum.

The Personal/Corporate Banking segment saw a YoY decline, but the Global Wealth Management segment saw a +6% YoY growth, driven in part by "net new loans $3.4B driven by EMEA and Americas," which I think is a future upside factor as new loans made this year can drive future interest income.

So, my impression on growth factors discussed above are that on the one hand it seems UBS is innovating and also achieving results in a few segments driving upside potential, while also having some weakness in others and facing stiff competition in the US from peers as macro demand for financial advice continues. In this case, I am inclined to be on the low bullish side in the category of growth.

Profitability: Margins Trailing Key Peers, But Post-Merger Cost Cutting Still On Track

Next, let's talk about the topic of profitability.

With the next earnings call in a few weeks on Oct. 29th, for now as a run up to that we can touch on some points from the Q2 results.

Following trends in margins gives me some framework to understand how efficient this firm may be in generating profits, so in this case we can see the net income margin is 13.3%, much lower than the sector average.

Compared to similar peers, which I picked among global banks with some similar businesses including Morgan Stanley (MS), Deutsche Bank (DB), Barclay's (BCS), and Goldman Sachs (GS), UBS actually trails all of them on net margin:

I believe such data lowers the overall rating score for UBS, as it appears less efficient at profit generation than some key banking peers, with Goldman taking the helm in this category.

To be fair, it is understandable that UBS also went through a major integration after its takeover of Credit Suisse a while back, and it is still an ongoing process, with France24 reporting back in February that "it had achieved 58 percent of its target of achieving $13 billion in cost savings by the end of next year as part of the merger."

Further, in the Q2 results media release, UBS, mentioned that it "delivered further USD $0.7B in exit rate gross cost saves bringing cumulative cost reductions to USD $9.1B, or 70% of the USD ~$13B in expected gross saves."

So, on the one hand it seems that UBS could use a boost to its profit margin and thereby help drive future profitability growth, but it also has achieved considerable savings in the wake of the merger so that should be mentioned. Further, the analyst consensus points to 18 - 30% YoY EPS growth in the next two years, another positive point.

Given the combination of these factors, I am inclined towards a hold rating in this category.

Balance Sheet & Credit Rating: Top Ratings From Agencies, Although Loan Loss Reserves Creeping Up

Moving along, next we will discuss some elements of balance sheet risk.

Notable to mention is that from the UBS website we can see some strong credit ratings from major agencies, of the parent company itself, UBS Group AG:

That should add extra confidence to investors concerned about credit risk, but also it is expected that a bank like UBS will be watched closely as it is listed as one of global systemically important banks in the Financial Stability Board's 2024 list. Earlier, I also mentioned how Swiss regulators are cracking down further with new rules that UBS will have to abide by as well.

When it comes to debt vs equity, UBS with a D/E of 4.48 is on the higher end of the peer group I created below, perhaps indicating a higher reliance on debt and some investors may consider this an added risk:

From an additional angle, let's consider a key metric in banks and that is the CET1 ratio. In its Q2 results release, UBS had the following optimistic news:

In the second quarter, we maintained a strong capital position with a CET1 capital ratio of 14.4% and a CET1 leverage ratio of 4.4%. Both are in excess of our guidance of ~14% and >4.0%, respectively, and provide a solid capital buffer to requirements during the integration, while allowing us to self-fund strategic investments and return capital to shareholders.

Further, in terms of the balance sheet we should consider asset risk exposure. The largest asset on the balance sheet seems to be loans this bank made to others, to the tune of +$667B worth of them. This also means the bank can be exposed to loan losses.

We can see, for instance, that balance sheet data on Seeking Alpha shows that the allowance for loan losses, which is like a reserve the bank sets aside for anticipated losses, has seen a rising trend from March 2024 through June 2025, painting a picture of slightly elevated loan loss risk potential:

So, given the strong credit ratings and CET1, but also the elevated D/E and the growing trend of loan loss reserves, I am more inclined to saty in the neutral camp in this category.

Cashflow & Dividend Trends: A Dividend Growth Recovery, And Mixed Cashflow Picture

So far, this stock has been in the neutral/hold range across the board, so let's see if the topic of cashflow and dividend trends swings the needle either way.

With a below-average dividend yield of just around 2.2%, and only paying out annually rather than quarterly, I don't think it makes the case for high-yield investors seeking regular, frequent income. So, then it is a question of whether it is a dividend growth stock, and in that realm it has improved.

Consider that it reversed its dividend incline from 2021 to now be on a growth trend again, with a 5 year dividend CAGR of nearly +20%.

This is how that looks visually:

In terms of a cashflow metric tied to the core business, the cashflow from operations, we can see from my chart below that it has seen a considerable downward trend into negative territory over the last 2 years:

However, the nature of a major bank like this is such that it can also easily raise cash by selling tradeable securities/investments, and also borrowing via debt, which it seems to do quite a bit of. For instance, cashflow data shows in the quarter ending March it raised around $35B in cash from financing.

Therefore, I think the trends above should be taken in the context of the industry it is in. A REIT, for instance, may be sitting on a few billion of illiquid assets like property that it cannot easily convert to cash, whereas a bank like this can sell off a portion of its liquid securities (such as fixed-income assets) each trading day and raise cash, even though sometimes it may have to sell those securities at a loss.

So, the above dividend and cashflow case presents at least a neutral/hold justification at this point.

Valuation & Share Price: A Neutral View on Future Upside vs Current Price/Valuation

Coming around the corner now to the finish line, let's consider a forward-looking valuation metric and also talk about future price targets.

This stock's fwd P/E (GAAP-based) is 18.10, much higher than the sector avg. of 11.57, indicating some overvaluation is there.

In addition to the elevated valuation, along with the bullish momentum vs the S&P500 which I mentioned earlier, we can see also in the price chart I made below that the stock is trading lately well above its 200-day simple moving average:

So, there is not a mismatch between valuation and price since both are elevated.

Considering the positive analyst consensus on EPS growth for FY25 and FY26, I can see what could motivate the bulls to go after this stock, but at the same time I also showed in this article how UBS trails key peers in profit margins. This bullishness suggests the market may be expecting considerable margin improvement going ahead, particularly after the Credit Suisse integration fully completes finally.

In terms of future price targets, the Wall St consensus is saying an avg target of $41.16, which is only a +5.5% upside potential.

My own target is $42.17 (based on fwd P/E x consensus EPS estimate for Dec 2025), and this would be an upside of around +8% vs Wednesday afternoon's trading.

Considering the elevated valuation and share price, but also the potential price upside forecast of less than 10%, and my mixed sentiment about the ability of UBS to catch up to key global banking peers on profit margins, I am in the hold/neutral camp in this category too.

Conclusion: Agree With Consensus Across The Board

In conclusion, my own research today led to the exact same across-the-board rating of hold today in the consensus (Wall St, SA analysts, quant system).

Today's article presented 5 different categories which each supported a lukewarm/middle-of-the-road approach to this stock, reaffirming my prior sentiment on UBS yet again.

Don't fret, however, as a hold rating is not a negative outlook, but more of a cautious optimism looking ahead.

To consider a risk to my neutral/cautiously optimistic view of this stock, that has not yet been mentioned in this article or rating score, I would say a challenge could come from central bank decisions impacting interest rates. A continuation of elevated interest rates could negatively impact bond values, and earlier I've shown that this bank holds a lot of securities. However, it could also impact demand for loans, and this bank has a huge loan book it seems.

On the contrary, an extended period of low interest rates in the next several years could boost underlying bond values in their portfolio, and the cost of borrowing, so because we don't know too far in advance what central banks will decide for now it is a potential future risk investors will have to anticipate not just at UBS but at similar firms engaged in lending as well as holding large amounts of fixed-income securities.

data.title: MindWalk Holdings: Repositioned TechBio Platform With Early LensAI Proof Points
data.description:
Thesis

MindWalk Holdings (NASDAQ:HYFT), formerly ImmunoPrecise Antibodies, has completed a strategic pivot from a service-oriented CRO toward an asset-light, software-led techbio platform.

MindWalk’s core technology HYFT and commercial LensAI suite aim to compress discovery timelines and improve decision quality before wet lab spend.

Early outputs, such as an AI-generated GLP-1 agonist program with a dual-action (longevity-linked) pathway, identification of a universal dengue epitope candidate, and in silico immunogenicity [ADA] risk screening illustrate LensAI’s practical range.

With last-reported record quarterly sales of C$7.6 million, which is +45% YoY, a stronger balance sheet following the Netherlands divestiture, and retirement of a Yorkville debenture, MindWalk appears to enter a next phase with clearer operating focus.

The stock has moved up about 190% over the past year. I believe that at a $75 million valuation, the company is still quite undervalued. This the first coverage of the company on Seeking Alpha.

MindWalk Holdings

MindWalk Holdings currently operates under a single identity focused on in silico discovery and data products rather than capital-intensive wet lab services currently has three subsidiaries: ImmunoPrecise Antibodies, BioStrand and Talem. The company has transitioned from lower-margin services to a platform that monetizes via SaaS/DaaS, collaborations, and asset out-licensing.

HYFT is its main technology, and LensAI the AI-based drug development/discovery suite based on that technology. There is initial evidence that LensAI can generate candidate-level insights in metabolic and infectious disease while reducing safety risk through early immunogenicity screening. I will explain some sector- or company-specific terminology upfront.

Terminology

The company’s current focus is on in-silico discovery of therapeutics. ‘In-silico’ means that the drug discovery is computational; after the divestiture of the Dutch division, the company no longer has an actual lab or ‘wet lab’. This divestiture also allowed the company to focus on what it is most known for. HYFT is the technology developed within Biostrand by Belgian entrepreneur Dirk Van Hyfte. A ‘HYFT’ is a pattern in biology that represents the structure of that part of biology and its function. LensAI is the commercially ready software suite [API] which incorporates the HYFT technology, has been launched in June 2024 received the 2024 Impact Award, with a commercial release in October 2024.

Epitope mapping is the process of mapping binding sites on proteins. Epitope binning refers to the process of grouping epitopes based on similar binding sites. Multi-omics is a concept used to express the connectivity and integrated analysis of multiple elements of the study of biology, such as genomics, proteomics, metabolomics or transcriptomics.

MindWalk

Introduction

MindWalk represents a shift from services to an integrated platform-driven bio-native AI company.

MindWalk’s focus is on offering AI tools for drug and therapy development that takes a from-the-ground-up approach and tries to integrate multi-omics to create a knowledge graph of biology.

The HYFT technology is the foundation for that approach. HYFT is trained on various information including scientific literature, patient records and lab data. The HYFT technology encompasses 660 million HYFTs, 20 million structural HYFTs and entails 25 billion biologic connections.

The goal of the HYFTs is to feed the AI learning model with something more than raw sequences of data, which should allow the model to improve epitope discovery, perform and improve immunogenicity screening, make accurate predictions about the safety and efficacy of future drugs, better predict drug-drug interaction, and improve the design process of future drugs.

LensAI integrates DNA-RNA sequence protein structure which is the focus of other learning models such as the well-known AlphaFold, and text.

Business model

The business model of MindWalk is diverse.

Pharmaceutical or other companies can access the LensAI platform by paying a subscription fee. Additional analyses and usage of LensAI may lead to higher costs.

MindWalk also co-develops or outlicenses internally-developed drugs, which may lead to upfront payments, milestone payments or royalties.

MindWalk counts 19 out 20 big pharma companies among its total of over 750 active customers.

Compared to the traditional and currently divested business, LensAI drives higher margins of 90%.

One of the recent announcements in respect of the company’s core-business related to a partnership for the AI-based development of novel cancer therapeutics with a global biotechnology leader. The partnership is valued at $8 million initially, with the potential to expand to $10 million.

ImmunoPrecise Antibodies [IPA]

ImmunoPrecise Antibodies is the primary working company which serves customers in their pursuit to discover antibody and develop therapies and drug, integrating LensAI and HYFT.

BioStrand

BioStrand is a Belgian subsidiary acquired for approximately €20 million in March 2022, integrates the HYFT technology, including computational tools, knowledge graphs and LensAI.

A more detailed breakdown of LENSai’s potential in epitope mapping and binning, protein structure prediction, immunogenicity screening and antibody development can be found in an presentation presented at the occasion of MindWalk’s 2024 tech day.

Talem

Talem is focused on discovering next-generation monoclonal antibodies for a variety of diseases. Talem’s business model is both in-house and partnering. Talem can use IPA and BioStrand’s technology.

This is Talem’s pipeline, with some drug candidates having been partnered to companies such as Biotheus, Pierre Fabre and OmniAB:

Evidence of execution

In 2025, MindWalk highlighted several LensAI-generated outputs.

In metabolic disease, LensAI produced GLP-1-like constructs optimized for binding, stability, and function within some weeks. Third-party in vitro data reportedly showed semaglutide-class receptor activation, and subsequent analysis suggested a second, longevity-linked pathway that could differentiate the program.

In infectious disease, LensAI identified a single epitope shared across all four dengue serotypes - a potential foundation for a universal vaccine approach - described as structurally stable, immunologically active, and accessible, with preclinical work advancing.

LensAI’s immunogenicity module screens across ~900 HLA variants and the human proteome to flag ADA risk early, with case study performance supportive of prospective utility.

The platform has also predicted binding sites on unseen antibody-antigen complexes without wet lab inputs.

Collectively, these data suggest LensAI can both generate hypotheses and reduce downstream risk.

Financials

At the end of the last quarter, MindWalk reported a record C$7.6 million quarterly revenue, representing 45% growth year-over-year, with 53% margins. This graph shows a comparison with quarters from the years before.

Gross profit was C$4 million, and net loss improved to C$3 million compared to C$4 million the year before. Adjusted EBITDA loss narrowed to C$1.4 million as compared to C$2.8 million the year before.

At the end of the last quarter, the company had C$5 million of cash. Adding C$16.1 million in net proceeds from the divestiture of IPA Netherlands, i.e. $12 million, the company had pro forma C$21 million in cash.

The Yorkville debenture has also been retired.

Given last reported net cash used in operating activities of C$4.2 million and net cash used in investing activities of C$594 million, cash burn for the past quarter was C$4.8 million. On that basis, one could assume that the current cash should be able to provide runway for the company until at least mid-2026.

Because divested operations contributed to prior revenue, I do not assume immediate sequential revenue growth. LensAI bookings, mix and margin and collaboration structure expenses are what I am looking for in next quarter’s results. Margins should improve as the in silico discovery arm has much higher margins. In that sense, I am curious to see the company set out its path to profitability in the years ahead.

Valuation

MindWalk has gone through a number of changes, given the separate businesses, and its recent divestiture. At this time, the market may still value MindWalk as a legacy services business, while its operating model and margin structure increasingly resemble software-led techbio platforms.

LensAI is presumably the most value-generating element of the company’s business. LensAI was only recently commercially launched. As a new AI-tool with high margins – BioStrand has over 90% gross margins - which should still gain awareness with the wider audience, I expect this to be adopted more broadly in the coming years. is in the early stages of commercial development. I believe investors may want to look at it as both a revenue-generating API and a potential pipeline in a software product. Though the initial launch of LensAI did not have much effect on the stock price, the announcements as of June 2025 related to GLP-1 and dengue did seem to have a meaningful impact on the stock.

In that regard, it is noteworthy to note that BioStrand’s growth of revenue went from C$37,627 for fiscal year 2023 to C$1,040,152 for fiscal year 2025.

Simple EV/Sales and Price/Sales screens favor MindWalk, while LensAI remains early in its commercial cycle. My comparison here is with other techbio companies such as Absci Corporation (ABSI) primarily, and Recursion Pharmaceuticals (RXRX) to a lesser extent. These are more known techbio companies, but I believe that with LensAI, MindWalk is playing in the same league.

Absci, for example, currently has a $583 million valuation. It recorded C$30.6 million in net losses in the last quarter, and had C$117.5 million in cash at the end of that quarter. It does not have an ongoing business, and its AI-generated pipeline [slide 12] is still early-stage, with one of four visible drug candidate in Phase 1 studies and the rest in earlier stages of development.

Recursion has a $2.56 billion market cap, with 6 internal drug candidates four of which are in Phase 1 studies, and with further visible partnering and milestone payments with some big pharma companies. Recursion is, by all means, the most visible techbio company in the space, operating the most powerful supercomputer in the pharmaceutical industry and having partnered with Nvidia (NVDA) to that purpose. Nonetheless, while last-reported cash was at $533.8, its expected runway is only until Q4 2027, as quarterly revenue was only $19.2 million and net loss was $171.9 million.

Roivant Sciences (ROIV), which I have covered here before, is in my eyes actually the most successful techbio company, with many subsidiaries and an interesting and further advanced pipeline and a $11.2 billion valuation.

Given the two discoveries this year so far related to GLP-1 and dengue, I assume that it will not be long until LensAI generates a sufficiently large pipeline to partner or to build on internally. Both the GLP-1 and dengue program have large addressable markets, of $53 billion and $2.2 billion respectively.

From a perspective of valuation metrics such as EV/sales and price/sales, I believe that MindWalk is strongly undervalued compared to the other companies I have mentioned above. Data originate from Seeking Alpha’s valuation page for the respective companies.

I believe that MindWalk is heavily undervalued compared to some more visible techbio players.

Risks

Key risks include competition from alternative AI-enabled discovery approaches (including internal programs), and the uncertainty inherent in translating in silico insights into preclinical and clinical outcomes. MindWalk may prioritize partnerships to fund later stages, which could affect timelines and economics.

The progress of AI should not be underestimated, and there are several visible competitors of the company, and probably several internal programs within big pharmaceutical companies that may compete or come to compete with LensAI in the future. The competitivity of the company in the long term is therefore not guaranteed.

While I believe that the reports on LensAI’s output are primarily interesting to customers because of the quality of output generated, it is not excluded that at a later stage, these programs are not partnered or may be discontinued by the company. The company may report disappointing results at a given stage.

While AI clearly cuts development time, and I believe LensAI demonstrates that, preclinical research still needs to be done and time in the clinic will still be needed to ascertain the efficacy of any given drug.

While liquidity improved post-divestiture, the company may not have sufficient cash to push all of its interesting drug candidates forward, and may need to rely on potential partnering to do so. As a result, the company may announce delays with regards to certain assets.

Conclusion

After its recent rebranding and divestiture of the Netherlands wet lab entity, I believe Mindwalk Holdings has pivoted to a platform company with a commercial software suite (LensAI) designed to de-risk and accelerate discovery ahead of wet lab spend as its primary commercial asset. LensAI’s early outputs are the generation of a dual-action GLP-1 agonist, and the basis for a universal dengue vaccine epitope, which appears to be safe, immunologically active, and structurally stable.

The company has an ongoing revenue-generating business which it aims to substantially grow the coming years. LensAI and its HYFT technology are now being used in MindWalk’s different companies, with ImmunoPrecise and BioStrand those in which that software suite should be developed mostly in the coming years. Talem has its own pipeline of drug candidates and is now also using that technology.

With a cleaner balance sheet after the divestiture of its Netherlands wet lab entity and a high-margin software suite, I believe the current valuation undervalues the company’s platform and partnering potential. The company has many partners, including 19 out of 20 big pharma companies, and I am looking forward to how the future revenue/profitability mix would look like in the coming quarters.

For the above reasons, I believe a Buy rating is in place at this time.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold Trading Like A Meme Or Pricing Doomsday, Either Way I'm Out
data.description:
Key point

The fundamentals are positive for Gold (NYSEARCA:GLD), but the recent price action has been speculative, and highly correlated with the retail-favored meme stocks. Thus, I'm downgrading gold to a Hold from a Buy.

Gold - fundamental valuation

The fundamental value of any financial asset can be estimated as the present value of future cash flows. This applies to stocks, bonds, private businesses, and even cash-producing real estate.

Gold is technically a commodity, and it does not produce any cash flow, thus, it cannot be valued based on traditional metrics. The fundamental value of gold is based on supply and demand. Given that gold is not an industrial commodity, the demand for gold is primarily based on investment demand.

Specifically, investors consider gold as: 1) an effective hedge against inflation, 2) hedge against depreciating US dollar, 3) hedge against negative real interest rates, which usually happens during deflationary recessions, 4) hedge against geopolitical instability, and finally 5) an ultimate hedge against the collapse of the system, or as a doomsday investment.

Since 1970, gold really had only 4 major spikes: 1) late 1970s, 2) 2001-2012, 3) 2019-2020, and 4) 2024-current, as the chart below shows.

Gold as a hedge against inflation

Gold proved to be an effective hedge against the inflationary spike during the late 1970s, early 1980s.

However, Gold has been essentially flat during the recent inflationary spike in 2022. Given these two inflationary episodes, the performance of Gold has been mixed as an inflation hedge.

Gold as a hedge against the falling US Dollar

During the inflationary spike of the late 1970s, the US Dollar also sharply depreciated, and thus gold was an effective hedge against the falling USD as well.

More importantly, gold also proved to be an effective hedge against the depreciating US Dollar from 2001 to 2012, and during this period inflation was low and stable.
